---
title: "Client Report - Star Wars for Dummies"
subtitle: "Unit 5 Task 3"
author: "Ella Patten"
format:
  html:
    self-contained: true
    page-layout: full
    title-block-banner: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-location: body
    number-sections: false
    html-math-method: katex
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-overflow: wrap
    code-copy: hover
    code-tools:
        source: false
        toggle: true
        caption: See code
execute: 
  warning: false
    
---

```{python}
import polars as pl 
import numpy as np
from lets_plot import *
import polars.selectors as cs
# imports for ML
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn import metrics
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.metrics import classification_report

LetsPlot.setup_html(isolated_frame=True)

data_url = "https://byuidatascience.github.io/DS250-Course-Polars/Data/StarWars_with_emails.csv"
```

<!-- The first 10 rows are not part of the datset we need.

The columns headers are split across two rows. We need to clean and simplify the column headers-->

```{python}
# Read in and tidy up the first row
a = pl.Series(pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows = 10, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding = 'utf8-lossy').row(0))
# Note the non-aschii character in the column "Do you consider yourself to be a fan of the Expanded Universe". (column AF if you open it in Excel). 

# BTW: Detecting what it is and where can be difficult

# Lossy means that invalid utf8 values are replaced with � characters. When using other encodings than utf8 or utf8-lossy, the input is first decoded in memory with python. Defaults to utf8...ignore_errors = True does not replace the non-utf8 character with a blank space like you would hope

a[2] = "seen_any" #simply overwrite the value at index 2

# Rather than changing each column header manually,
# here we use regex to apply changes more efficiently
a = (
  a.str.replace(r".+seen.+", "seen_")
   .str.replace(r".+rank.+", "rank_")
   .str.replace(r"(.+favorably.+)", "fav_")
   .fill_null(strategy = "forward")

   .str.replace("\uFFFD+", "") #this gets rid of the question marks in a diamond characters 
   .str.strip_chars()
   .str.to_lowercase()
   .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
   .str.replace(r"location(.+)", "location")

)
a[3] = "star_wars_fan"
a[30] = "who_shot_first"
a[31] = "know_expanded_universe"
a[32] = "expanded_universe_fan"
a[33] = "star_trek_fan"
```

<!-- Combine series q and a into one series to be used as column names-->

```{python}
b = pl.Series(pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows = 11, n_rows=1, has_header=False, encoding = 'utf8-lossy').row(0))

b = (
  b.str.replace(r"Star Wars: (Episode \w+) .+", "${1}")
   .str.replace("Response", "")
   .str.replace_all(" ", "_")
   .fill_null("")
   .str.strip_chars()
)

```

```{python}
#Now combine the two series, each of which contains part of the desired column header
new_names = (a + b).to_list()

# Read in the data, skipping over the column header rows
# and use 'new_names' as the column headers
df = pl.read_csv(data_url, skip_rows=12, has_header=False, new_columns = new_names )
```


## Executive Summary
 - This is a high level overview of the analysis, including the problem statement, the data used, and the main findings. It should be concise and to the point, summarizing the key insights/recommendations without going into too much detail. These are usually bullet points, no graphs. PLEASE EXPLICITLY STATE IN THIS SECTION WHETHER YOU DID THE STRETCH OR NOT!

_type your write-up and analysis here_



## Methods

this is a high level overview of the methods used in the analysis. This should include an explanation of data preparation steps, especially how you dealt with each categorical variable and why. Showing the resulting or original distribution of variables may be helpful. Describe how you handled missing values and outliers. How did/might your decisions impact results? Explain any feature engineering steps you took. This is also where you will put the graphs you recreated to show you validated the dataset and that it matches the article.

Regex is used to parse the email column and extract each respondent's first name, last name, and email domain into separate columns. Each pattern uses markers in the email — such as the start of a string and the @ symbol — to isolate the relevant text.
```{python}
# Extract first name, last name, and email domain from the email column
df = df.with_columns([
    pl.col("email").str.strip_chars().str.extract(r"^([a-zA-Z]+)", 1).alias("first_name"),
    pl.col("email").str.strip_chars().str.extract(r"([a-zA-Z]+)@", 1).alias("last_name"),
    pl.col("email").str.strip_chars().str.extract(r"@([a-zA-Z0-9]+)", 1).alias("email_domain"),
])
df.select(["email", "first_name", "last_name", "email_domain"]).head(5)
```

Here we count the email domains, normalizing all domains to lowercase before grouping, since the raw data contains inconsistent capitalization. This ensures that variations of the same domain are counted together rather than treated as separate entries.
```{python}
# Unique email domain count
domain_count = (df.with_columns(pl.col("email_domain").str.to_lowercase()).group_by("email_domain").len().sort("len", descending=True))
domain_count
```

The dataset is filtered to respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film. Rather than relying on the self-reported `seen_any` column, we check the six individual film columns directly — if any are non-null, the respondent has seen at least one film.
```{python}
# Respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film
seen_cols = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("seen_") and c != "seen_any"]
df_seen = df.filter(
    pl.any_horizontal([pl.col(c).is_not_null() for c in seen_cols])
)

print(df_seen.head(5))
```


Here we pull apart the answers that are numbers yet currently formatted as strings and turn them into numbers. When it's a range the data point set is the lowest number in the range. The categories must be numeric for machine learning, so each category is mapped to an integer. This preserves the ordering of each variable and keeps the conversion simple and consistent. Now we can use 'df_for_ml' as our machine learning ready data set!

```{python}
# Age
age_map = {"18-29": 18, "30-44": 30, "45-60": 45, "> 60": 60}

df_for_ml = (
    df_seen
    .with_columns(pl.col("age").replace(age_map).cast(pl.Int32).alias("age_num"))
    .drop("age")
)
# Education
education_ = {
    "Less than high school degree": 1,
    "High school degree": 2,
    "Some college or Associate degree": 3,
    "Bachelor degree": 4,
    "Graduate degree": 5,
}

df_for_ml = (
    df_for_ml
    .with_columns(pl.col("education").replace(education_).cast(pl.Int32).alias("education_num"))
    .drop("education")
)
# Income
income = {
    "$0 - $24,999": 0,
    "$25,000 - $49,999": 25000,
    "$50,000 - $99,999": 50000,
    "$100,000 - $149,999": 100000,
    "$150,000+": 150000,
}

df_for_ml = (
    df_for_ml
    .with_columns(pl.col("household_income").replace(income).cast(pl.Int64).alias("income_num"))
    .drop("household_income")
)
# Favorability
fav = {
    "Very favorably": 5,
    "Somewhat favorably": 4,
    "Neither favorably nor unfavorably (neutral)": 3,
    "Somewhat unfavorably": 2,
    "Very unfavorably": 1,
    "Unfamiliar (N/A)": 0,
}

fav_columns = [c for c in df.columns if c.startswith("fav_")]

df_for_ml = (
    df_for_ml
    .with_columns([pl.col(c).replace(fav).cast(pl.Int32) for c in fav_columns])
)

# Drop identifier columns
df_for_ml = df_for_ml.drop(["respondentid", "first_name", "last_name", "email"])

# Household income 
df_for_ml = df_for_ml.with_columns(
    (pl.col("income_num") >= 50000).cast(pl.Int8).alias("high_income")
)

# One-hot encode remaining strings
str_cols = df_for_ml.select(cs.string()).columns
df_for_ml = df_for_ml.to_dummies(columns=str_cols)

# show head of complete data set 
df_for_ml.select(["age_num", "education_num", "income_num"] + fav_columns).head(5)
```

Here we're able to create our Machine Learning model to predict if a respondent's household income is over $50,000. We remove the rows where the respondents didn't record their income.Then we choose our features and target. We then split the data 70/30 and created our model. The model's accuracy is shown below. 

```{python}
# Remove rows where income was not reported
df_model = df_for_ml.filter(pl.col("high_income").is_not_null())

# Set features
feature_cols = [c for c in df_model.columns if c not in ["high_income", "income_num"]]

features = df_model.select(feature_cols).fill_null(0).to_numpy()
target = df_model.select("high_income").to_numpy().ravel()

x_train, x_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(features, target, test_size=0.3)

# Create model
model = RandomForestClassifier()
model.fit(x_train, y_train)

y_pred = model.predict(x_test)

acc = metrics.accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred)
print('accuracy:', acc)
```

## Results

_type your report here, you will likely want multiple code chunks and text intermingled_


Here is the shape of the table for all the respondents who have seen at least one film, adding up to a total of 835.
```{python}
# Shape of table for respondents who have seen at least one Star Wars film
print(df_seen.shape)
```

Our model's accuracy is shown below. The classification report shows the precision, recall, and f1-score for each class. 
```{python}
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred, target_names=["< $50k", ">= $50k"]))
```
